name: Plaid API
what_it_does: Plaid connects applications to users' bank accounts, enabling retrieval of transaction history, account balances, income verification, identity data, and ACH payment initiation. It aggregates data from thousands of financial institutions via a unified API.
use_cases: Retrieving bank transaction history for personal finance management
Account balance verification before initiating payments
Income and employment verification for lending applications
ACH payment initiation and bank account verification
Identity verification using bank account ownership data
Cash flow analysis for underwriting and credit decisions
not_for: Real-time trading or brokerage account management (use Alpaca or Coinbase)
International bank connectivity outside supported countries (primarily US, Canada, UK, EU)
Applications needing free or low-cost bank data access (pricing is high per item)
best_when: An agent needs to read user bank transactions, verify account ownership, or initiate ACH transfers as part of a financial application.
avoid_when: Cost sensitivity is high (at ~$1.50/item/mo, costs scale quickly with users) or when bank connectivity is needed in unsupported geographies.

auth.notes: client_id and secret for API authentication. User bank connections produce access_tokens (per institution per user) via Link flow. access_tokens are persistent and long-lived. Sandbox, development, and production environments use separate credentials. No fine-grained API scopes — access_token grants access to all products enabled for that Item.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Sandbox environment is free and unlimited for testing
pricing.paid_tiers: No monthly platform fee; per-call pricing varies by product. Transactions: ~$0.30/call + $1.50/item/mo. Auth: ~$1.00/call. Identity: ~$1.00/call. Volume discounts negotiated with sales.
pricing.notes: No free production tier. Pricing is opaque — exact rates require contacting sales for volume. Can become expensive quickly at scale. Development environment has limited calls per day.

performance.rate_limits: Rate limits vary by endpoint and plan tier. /transactions/get has lower limits than balance checks. Exact limits provided post-signup. Sandbox is effectively unlimited.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Plaid uses structured error objects with error_type, error_code, error_message, and display_message. Error codes are well-documented and categorized (INVALID_REQUEST, INVALID_INPUT, INSTITUTION_ERROR, etc.). display_message is user-facing safe text. Machine-readable codes enable reliable agent error branching.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No idempotency keys on API calls. The Link flow (user-facing) is the main non-idempotent risk area; API read calls are naturally idempotent but write operations (payment initiation) are not.
known_agent_gotchas: Link token flow is user-interactive (browser-based) — agents cannot automate the initial bank connection without user involvement
access_tokens are per-institution per-user; a user with 3 banks = 3 separate tokens to manage
Transaction data can be 1-2 days delayed from real-time; not suitable for real-time balance decisions
ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED errors require the user to re-authenticate via Link — agents cannot resolve this autonomously
Transactions sync is eventual — initial historical load can take minutes to hours for new items
Institution errors are frequent and unpredictable; agents must handle INSTITUTION_ERROR gracefully
Webhook delivery is not guaranteed exactly-once — agents must be idempotent on webhook processing
Production access requires Plaid to approve your use case — sandbox success does not guarantee production approval

score_rationale: Plaid has well-structured error codes and good SDK coverage, making it relatively agent-friendly for read operations. However, the critical limitation for agents is that the initial bank connection (Link flow) is inherently user-interactive and cannot be automated. Ongoing token management, institution errors requiring user re-auth, and eventual consistency of transaction data add operational complexity. Strong choice when user bank connectivity is the goal; poor fit for fully autonomous workflows.
